title: Redis L2 cache without L1 — no avalanche protection when Redis goes down
expected_feedback: Standard: Service relies exclusively on Redis (L2) with no in-process cache layer. When Redis becomes unavailable (outage, network partition) or keys mass-expire at the same TTL boundary, all 15K QPS hit the database directly — a classic cache avalanche. Additional issues: (1) TTL is fixed at 30 minutes with no jitter, so keys inserted in bursts expire simultaneously; (2) Redis errors return immediately with no degradation path — every client gets an error. Fix: add an L1 in-process cache (ristretto, groupcache, or sync.Map for simple cases) with short TTL (5-10s) in front of Redis — L1 absorbs load during Redis outages and smooths expiry spikes. Add TTL jitter (30min +/- 10-20%). Add a circuit breaker on the Redis path so that when Redis is down, requests fall through to DB with rate limiting rather than returning errors.
